value.Title: DOE awards $36M to North Dakota research center for enhanced oil recovery technologies

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJP-SD43-RW3M-72C1-00000-00DOE awards $36M to North Dakota research center for enhanced oil recovery technologies2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:08:30ZLexisNexisDOE awards $36M to North Dakota research center for enhanced oil recovery technologies

The U.S. Department of Energy’s (DOE) is awarding $36 million in federal funding for the University of North Dakota’s Energy & Environmental Research Center.

The grant, provided through the DOE’s Hydrocarbons and Geothermal Energy Office (HGEO), is to advance the commercial deployment of enhanced oil recovery (EOR) technologies in the Bakken shale formation. The Bakken Enhanced Oil Recovery–Cracking the Code program will generate critical data and insights to enable efficient, large-scale implementation of carbon dioxide-based enhanced oil recovery (EOR).

“North Dakota has proven itself to be a leader in energy innovation, and the Bakken Enhanced Oil Recovery initiative builds on that legacy,” DOE Assistant Secretary for the Hydrocarbons and Geothermal Energy Office Kyle Haustveit said. “This program is essential to maximize the full potential of our valuable hydrocarbon resources in the Bakken. By ‘cracking the code,’ these integrated pilot projects will help establish a clear path for the broad commercial deployment of enhanced energy recovery across the nation. The Bakken formation holds the potential to unlock billions of barrels of oil—resources that can power energy independence for generations to come.”

The Bakken holds substantial potential for increased oil recovery. Currently, only about 10 percent of the oil in unconventional shale formations is typically recovered. The Bakken program is designed to evaluate EOR strategies, potentially unlocking billions of additional barrels of incremental oil. It could also extend the life of the state’s coal-fired power plants by utilizing their captured carbon dioxide for EOR.

Each pilot project within the program will evaluate different injection strategies, reservoir conditions, and operational approaches to help establish a technical foundation for future commercial-scale deployment. Advanced AI and machine-learning tools will be used to identify operational insights, best practices, and critical success factors across all six pilots. This integrated approach ensures that individual pilot lessons are translated into actionable strategies for optimizing commercial-scale EOR deployment.

Along with the $36 million DOE investment, the project will receive $9 million in cost-share from the University of North Dakota’s Energy & Environmental Research Center and $100 million from state and private sources.

The post DOE awards $36M to North Dakota research center for enhanced oil recovery technologies appeared first on Daily Energy Insider.

urn:contentItem:6JJF-J1T3-S5VF-R02D-00000-00Department of Energy Awards $36 Million to EERC for Enhanced Oil Recovery Program2026-05-09T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:08:30ZLexisNexisDepartment of Energy Awards $36 Million to EERC for Enhanced Oil Recovery Program

Washington: Office of the Senator Kevin Cramer has issued the following news release:

Abundant oil and gas reserves, paired with first industry-leading fracking technology and affordable, reliable electricity, has put North Dakota at the forefront of U.S energy dominance. The U.S Department of Energy (DOE) is capitalizing on this advantage by investing $36 million to support the University of North Dakota ’ s Energy & Environmental Research Center ’ s (EERC) work improving enhanced oil recovery technologies in the Bakken formation.

“North Dakota is a model for the rest of the country and demonstrates energy production and energy innovation go hand in hand,” said U.S Senator Kevin Cramer (R-ND). “This investment recognizes years of work by the EERC, industry partners, and state leaders to unlock more of the Bakken ’ s enormous potential while utilizing captured carbon dioxide productively. We know the Bakken still holds billions of barrels of oil just out of reach of our current technology, but through the combined efforts of the Department of Energy and industry partners, those barrels of oil will enhance our national security and increase our economic growth. While there is still more work to be done, this announcement represents a commitment to the future of North Dakota ’ s oil industry—and a promise to America we will not step away from the resources God gave us. ”

Initial Bakken development only recovers approximately 10-15% of the oil in place. While partnerships between North Dakota and industry have made progress toward recovering additional oil, Cramer led efforts to correct leftist narratives regarding Bakken crude and leverage DOE dollars toward recovering the residual oil.

This federal investment includes $36 million from DOE ’ s Hydrocarbons and Geothermal Energy Office, along with $9 million in cost-share funding from the EERC and project partners for one of the six pilot projects.

urn:contentItem:6JJ7-HJG3-RX5S-81GW-00000-00Department of Energy Awards $36 Million to EERC for Enhanced Oil Recovery Program2026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:08:30ZLexisNexisU.S. SENATE DOCUMENTSDepartment of Energy Awards $36 Million to EERC for Enhanced Oil Recovery ProgramSen. Kevin Cramer (R-ND) News Release

BISMARCK, N.D. -- Abundant oil and gas reserves, paired with first industry-leading fracking technology and affordable, reliable electricity, has put North Dakota at the forefront of U.S. energy dominance. The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) is capitalizing on this advantage by investing $36 million to support the University of North Dakota's Energy and Environmental Research Center's (EERC) work improving enhanced oil recovery technologies in the Bakken formation.

"North Dakota is a model for the rest of the country and demonstrates energy production and energy innovation go hand in hand," said U.S. Senator Kevin Cramer (R-ND). "This investment recognizes years of work by the EERC, industry partners, and state leaders to unlock more of the Bakken's enormous potential while utilizing captured carbon dioxide productively. We know the Bakken still holds billions of barrels of oil just out of reach of our current technology, but through the combined efforts of the Department of Energy and industry partners, those barrels of oil will enhance our national security and increase our economic growth. While there is still more work to be done, this announcement represents a commitment to the future of North Dakota's oil industry--and a promise to America we will not step away from the resources God gave us."

Initial Bakken development only recovers approximately 10-15% of the oil in place. While partnerships between North Dakota and industry have made progress toward recovering additional oil, Cramer led efforts to correct leftist narratives regarding Bakken crude and leverage DOE dollars toward recovering the residual oil.

This federal investment includes $36 million from DOE's Hydrocarbons and Geothermal Energy Office, along with $9 million in cost-share funding from the EERC and project partners for one of the six pilot projects.

Read this original document at: https://www.cramer.senate.gov/news/press-releases/department-of-energy-awards-36-million-to-eerc-for-enhanced-oil-recovery-program

urn:contentItem:6JHW-38T3-S2TK-04T2-00000-00Energy Department to Invest $36 Million in Enhanced Oil Recovery Program at the University of North Dakota2026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:08:30ZLexisNexisEnergy Department to Invest $36 Million in Enhanced Oil Recovery Program at the University of North Dakota

Energy.gov

May 8th, 2026 ( Energy Dept. - Delivered by Newstex )

WASHINGTON-The U.S. Department of Energy's (DOE) Hydrocarbons and Geothermal Energy Office (HGEO) today announced the selection of a new project with $36 million in federal funding for the University of North Dakota's Energy & Environmental Research Center to advance the commercial deployment of enhanced oil recovery (EOR) technologies in the Bakken shale formation. Through an integration of laboratory, modeling, artificial intelligence (AI), and field-based activities, the Bakken Enhanced Oil Recovery-Cracking the Code (Bakken EOR-CC) program will generate critical data and insights to enable efficient, large-scale implementation of carbon dioxide-based EOR. These efforts support President Trump's commitment to American energy dominance by advancing technologies that increase domestic energy production and deliver affordable, reliable, and secure energy for the American people.

'North Dakota has proven itself to be a leader in energy innovation, and the Bakken Enhanced Oil Recovery initiative builds on that legacy,' saidDOE Assistant Secretary for the Hydrocarbons and Geothermal Energy Office Kyle Haustveit.'This program is essential to maximize the full potential of our valuable hydrocarbon resources in the Bakken.By 'cracking the code,' these integrated pilot projects will help establish a clear path for the broad commercial deployment of enhanced energy recovery across the nation.The Bakken formation holds the potential to unlock billions of barrels of oil-resources that can power energy independence for generations to come.'

The Bakken, a key unconventional tight oil play in North Dakota, holds substantial potential for increased oil recovery. Currently, only about 10% of the oil in unconventional shale formations is typically recovered. The Bakken EOR-CC program is designed to evaluate EOR strategies, potentially unlocking billions of additional barrels of incremental oil and extending the life of the state's coal-fired power plants by utilizing their captured carbon dioxide for EOR.

The program is uniquely positioned to capitalize on knowledge generated through six EOR pilot projects, five of which are state- and private-funded. Each pilot project will evaluate different injection strategies, reservoir conditions, and operational approaches to help establish a comprehensive technical foundation for future commercial-scale deployment. Advanced AI and machine-learning tools will be used to identify operational insights, best practices, and critical success factors across all six pilots to support broader deployment of enhanced oil recovery technologies across the Bakken shale formation. This integrated approach ensures that individual pilot lessons are translated into actionable strategies for optimizing commercial-scale EOR deployment. The Bakken EOR-CC program will establish a comprehensive technical foundation that transcends the scope of any single project.

Bakken EOR-CC will include DOE's investment of $36 million along with $9 million in cost-share from the University of North Dakota's Energy & Environmental Research Centerand project partners for one of the six pilot projects. State and private sources are providing approximately $100 million in funding for the remaining five pilot projects.

Learn about how HGEO is unleashing the full potential of America's hydrocarbon and geothermal resources to provide affordable, reliable, and secure energy here.

Link to the original story.

urn:contentItem:6JJ5-JJ73-RSM9-922W-00000-00Department of Energy Awards $36 Million to EERC for Enhanced Oil Recovery Program2026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:08:30ZLexisNexisDepartment of Energy Awards $36 Million to EERC for Enhanced Oil Recovery ProgramTargeted News ServiceWASHINGTON

Sen. Kevin Cramer, R-North Dakota, issued the following news release:

* * *

Department of Energy Awards $36 Million to EERC for Enhanced Oil Recovery Program

*

BISMARCK, N.D. -Abundant oil and gas reserves, paired with first industry-leading fracking technology and affordable, reliable electricity, has put North Dakota at the forefront of U.S. energy dominance. The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) is capitalizing on this advantage by investing $36 million to support the University of North Dakota's Energy & Environmental Research Center's (EERC) work improving enhanced oil recovery technologies in the Bakken formation.

"North Dakota is a model for the rest of the country and demonstrates energy production and energy innovation go hand in hand," said U.S. Senator Kevin Cramer (R-ND). "This investment recognizes years of work by the EERC, industry partners, and state leaders to unlock more of the Bakken's enormous potential while utilizing captured carbon dioxide productively. We know the Bakken still holds billions of barrels of oil just out of reach of our current technology, but through the combined efforts of the Department of Energy and industry partners, those barrels of oil will enhance our national security and increase our economic growth. While there is still more work to be done, this announcement represents a commitment to the future of North Dakota's oil industry-and a promise to America we will not step away from the resources God gave us."

Initial Bakken development only recovers approximately 10-15% of the oil in place. While partnerships between North Dakota and industry have made progress toward recovering additional oil, Cramer led efforts to correct leftist narratives regarding Bakken crude and leverage DOE dollars toward recovering the residual oil.

This federal investment includes $36 million from DOE's Hydrocarbons and Geothermal Energy Office, along with $9 million in cost-share funding from the EERC and project partners for one of the six pilot projects.

***

urn:contentItem:6JJ1-R213-RTBX-R1C1-00000-00Energy Department to Invest $36 Million in Enhanced Oil Recovery Program at the University of North Dakota2026-05-09T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:08:30ZLexisNexisEnergy Department to Invest $36 Million in Enhanced Oil Recovery Program at the University of North DakotaTargeted News ServiceWASHINGTON

The U.S. Department of Energy issued the following news release on May 8, 2026:

* * *

Energy Department to Invest $36 Million in Enhanced Oil Recovery Program at the University of North Dakota

By leveraging captured carbon dioxide, the project is set to unlock billions of additional barrels of oil from the Bakken shale formation

*

The U.S. Department of Energy's (DOE) Hydrocarbons and Geothermal Energy Office (HGEO) today announced the selection of a new project with $36 million in federal funding for the University of North Dakota's Energy & Environmental Research Center to advance the commercial deployment of enhanced oil recovery (EOR) technologies in the Bakken shale formation. Through an integration of laboratory, modeling, artificial intelligence (AI), and field-based activities, the Bakken Enhanced Oil Recovery-Cracking the Code (Bakken EOR-CC) program will generate critical data and insights to enable efficient, large-scale implementation of carbon dioxide-based EOR. These efforts support President Trump's commitment to American energy dominance by advancing technologies that increase domestic energy production and deliver affordable, reliable, and secure energy for the American people.

"North Dakota has proven itself to be a leader in energy innovation, and the Bakken Enhanced Oil Recovery initiative builds on that legacy," said DOE Assistant Secretary for the Hydrocarbons and Geothermal Energy Office Kyle Haustveit. "This program is essential to maximize the full potential of our valuable hydrocarbon resources in the Bakken. By 'cracking the code,' these integrated pilot projects will help establish a clear path for the broad commercial deployment of enhanced energy recovery across the nation. The Bakken formation holds the potential to unlock billions of barrels of oil--resources that can power energy independence for generations to come."

The Bakken, a key unconventional tight oil play in North Dakota, holds substantial potential for increased oil recovery. Currently, only about 10% of the oil in unconventional shale formations is typically recovered. The Bakken EOR-CC program is designed to evaluate EOR strategies, potentially unlocking billions of additional barrels of incremental oil and extending the life of the state's coal-fired power plants by utilizing their captured carbon dioxide for EOR.

The program is uniquely positioned to capitalize on knowledge generated through six EOR pilot projects, five of which are state- and private-funded. Each pilot project will evaluate different injection strategies, reservoir conditions, and operational approaches to help establish a comprehensive technical foundation for future commercial-scale deployment. Advanced AI and machine-learning tools will be used to identify operational insights, best practices, and critical success factors across all six pilots to support broader deployment of enhanced oil recovery technologies across the Bakken shale formation. This integrated approach ensures that individual pilot lessons are translated into actionable strategies for optimizing commercial-scale EOR deployment. The Bakken EOR-CC program will establish a comprehensive technical foundation that transcends the scope of any single project.

Bakken EOR-CC will include DOE's investment of $36 million along with $9 million in cost-share from the University of North Dakota's Energy & Environmental Research Center and project partners for one of the six pilot projects. State and private sources are providing approximately $100 million in funding for the remaining five pilot projects.

Learn about how HGEO is unleashing the full potential of America's hydrocarbon and geothermal resources to provide affordable, reliable, and secure energy here (https://www.energy.gov/hgeo/hydrocarbons-and-geothermal-energy-office).

* * *

urn:contentItem:6JHK-BKC3-S0RF-C55B-00000-00Federal Energy Regulatory Commission: Northern States Power Company, a Minnesota corporation submits tariff filing per 35.13(a)(2)(iii: 2026-05-05 Plum Creek E&P - 793 - 0.0.0 to be effective 4/28/2026 under ER26-2461 Filing Type : 102026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:35ZLexisNexisFederal Energy Regulatory Commission: Northern States Power Company, a Minnesota corporation submits tariff filing per 35.13(a)(2)(iii: 2026-05-05 Plum Creek E&P - 793 - 0.0.0 to be effective 4/28/2026 under ER26-2461 Filing Type : 10

Washington: Northern States Power Company, a Minnesota corporation has submitted the following document to the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission:

The Honorable Debbie-Anne A. ReeseSecretaryFederal Energy Regulatory Commission888 First Street, NEWashington, DC 20426RE: Northern States Power Company, a Minnesota corporationRevised Engineering & Procurement Agreement for MISO Project J2104Service Agreement No. 793-NSPDocket No. ER26-____-000Dear Secretary Reese:Pursuant to Section 205 of the Federal Power Act, 16 U.S.C § 824d (2018), and Section 35.13 of the regulations of the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (“Commission”), 18 C.F.R § 35. 13 (2026), Northern States Power Company, a Minnesota corporation (“NSPM”), hereby submits for filing the Revised Engineering and Procurement Agreement for MISO Project J2104 (“Revised Agreement”) between NSPM, Geronimo Power, LLC (“Geronimo”), and Midcontinent Independent System Operator, Inc. (“MISO”). Individually, NSPM, Geronimo, and MISO are referred to herein as “Party” and collectively as the “Parties”. The Agreement is submitted in accordance with Order No. 7141 and the Commission ’ s eTariff filing requirements.1. BackgroundNSPM and its utility operating company affiliate Northern States Power Company, a Wisconsin corporation (together the “NSP Companies”) are wholly owned utility operating company subsidiaries of Xcel Energy Inc., and are engaged in the business of generating, transmitting, distributing and selling electric power and energy and related services in the States of Minnesota, Michigan, North Dakota, South Dakota, and Wisconsin. The NSP Companies are transmission-owning members of the Midcontinent Independent System Operator, Inc. (“MISO”) and provide most transmission service pursuant to the MISO Open Access Transmission, Energy and Operating Reserve Markets Tariff on file with and accepted by the Commission. However, certain services are, or have been, provided pursuant to individual grandfathered agreements or the Xcel Energy Operating Companies Open Access Transmission Tariff (“Xcel Energy OATT”) on file with and accepted by the Commission.2 Individual NSPM service agreements are on file with the Commission in NSPM ’ s FERC Electric Tariff Second Revised Volume No. 3.1 Electronic Tariff Filings, Order No. 714, 124 FERC ¶ 61,270 (2008); order on clarification, Order No. 714-A, 147 FERC ¶ 61,115 (2014).2 The Xcel Energy OATT was restated in eTariff and accepted for filing in Public Service Company of Colorado, Docket No. ER16-1422-000, unpublished letter order (August 16, 2016).The Honorable Debbie-Anne A. ReeseMay 5, 2026Page 2 of 6Geronimo is a wholly owned subsidiary of National Grid Renewables, a leading renewable energy company with a nationwide development portfolio. Under National Grid Renewables ’ ownership, Geronimo has continued its core focus on project origination and early-stage development while leveraging broader construction, financing, and asset management capabilities.On April 28, 2026, the Parties executed the Revised Agreement modifying certain provisions of the Engineering and Procurement Agreement for MISO Project J2104 (Original Agreement). The Original Agreement was executed as a conforming agreement under Attachment W-1 of the Xcel Energy OATT, effective June 20, 2025.2. The Instant FilingThe modifications to the Original Agreement proposed herein reflect necessary changes that occurred following its execution, including an updated in-service date, an extension of the termination provision in absence of a Generator Interconnection Agreement, and an increase to the security amount associated with upgrades required to accommodate MISO Project J2104. In addition, the Revised Agreement includes minor administrative modifications that may be considered non-conforming. Accordingly, the Revised Agreement is being filed out of an abundance of caution.Section 14 and Appendix A identify the minor modifications that NSPM deems to be non-conforming. Consistent with filing requirements, NSPM submits the Revised Agreement in both redline and clean formats.3. Proposed Effective Date and Request for Waiver of NoticeNSPM respectfully requests that the Commission grant waiver of its sixty (60) day prior notice requirement3 and allow the Revised Agreement to become effective on April 28, 2026, the date of execution. NSPM submits that good cause exists to grant the requested waiver. It is the Commission ’ s policy to allow waiver of the prior notice requirements for service agreements under a tariff filed within thirty (30) days of the requested effective date.4 Here, the Revised Agreement is being filed within 7 days of its requested effective date of April 28, 2026. Moreover, the Parties support an April 28, 2026, effective date for the Revised Agreement. NSPM requests April 28, 2026, to provide certainty to the Parties as to the status of the Revised Agreement.4. Description of Documents Being Submitted with Filing5a. This transmittal letter,b. A clean version of Service Agreement No. 793-NSP, andc. A redline version of Service Agreement No. 793-NSP.3 18 C.F.R § 35.3(a) (2026).4 Prior Notice and Filing Requirements under Part II of the Federal Power Act, 64 FERC ¶ 61,139 at 61,984 (1993). See also PJM Interconnection, LLC, 194 FERC ¶ 61,096 at P 14 (2026).5 The Original Agreement was reported on the EQR as 778-NSP. This eTariff filing designates the Revised Agreement as 793-NSP.The Honorable Debbie-Anne A. ReeseMay 5, 2026Page 3 of 65. Communications and CorrespondenceCommunications and correspondence with respect to this filing should be directed to each of the following: 6Service and PostingAn electronic version or notice of this filing will be served upon each of the state commissions having jurisdiction over NSPM and on representatives of the Parties. A courtesy copy will be provided to the Division of Electric Power Regulation (Central). In addition, a copy of this filing will be posted at the offices of NSPM at 414 Nicollet Mall in Minneapolis, Minnesota.Please direct any questions regarding this filing to Elizabeth Walkup at (612) 216-8223.Respectfully submitted,/s/ Jonathan EserJonathan EserAssistant General Counsel & Senior Director, Transmission Business RelationsXcel Energy Services Inc.On behalf ofNorthern States Power Company, a Minnesota corporation6 NSPM requests waiver of Section 385.203(b)(3) of the Commission ’ s regulations to permit the designation of more than two persons upon whom service is to be made in this proceeding. 18 C.F.R § 385.203(b)(3) (2026).Jonathan EserAssistant General Counsel & Senior Director, Transmission Business RelationsXcel Energy Services Inc.701 Pennsylvania Ave. NW Suite 250Washington, DC 20004Email: Jonathan.Eser@xcelenergy.comMichèle B. BrüningPrincipal Transmission RepresentativeXcel Energy Services Inc.414 Nicollet Mall, 414-6Minneapolis, MN 55401Phone: (612) 330-5836Email: Michele.B.Bruning@xcelenergy.comJoseph W. LowellAssistant General CounselXcel Energy Services Inc.701 Pennsylvania Ave. NW Suite 250Washington, DC 20004Phone: (202) 661-4491Email: Joe.W.Lowell@xcelenergy.comSERVICE LISTMs. Natalie Tingle-StewartDirectorDivision of Electric Power Regulation (Central)Federal Energy Regulatory Commission888 First Street, NEWashington, DC 20426Email: natalie.tingle-stewart@ferc.govMr Mike BullActing Executive SecretaryMinnesota Public Utilities Commission121 7th Place E., Suite 350Saint Paul, MN 55101-2147Email: mike.bull@state.mn.usMs Patricia Van GerpenExecutive DirectorSouth Dakota Public Utilities Commission500 E. Capitol Ave.Pierre, SD 57501-5070Email: patty.vangerpen@state.sd.usMr Steve KahlExecutive SecretaryNorth Dakota Public Service Commission600 E Boulevard, Dept. 408Bismarck, ND 58505-0480Email: pscexecutivesec@nd.govChris AyikaInterconnection ManagerGeronimo Power8400 Normandale Lake Boulevard, Suite 1200Bloomington, MN 55437Email: cayika@geronimopower.com952-641-4031Kalen ColemanMidcontinent Independent System Operator, Inc.720 City Center Dr.Carmel, IN 46032Telephone: (317) 219-8481kcoleman@misoenergy.orgagreements@misoenergy.orgCERTIFICATE OF SERVICEI, Elizabeth Walkup, hereby certify that I have on this day served a notice of the enclosed filing via electronic mail on each party designated on the attached Service List.Dated at Minneapolis, Minnesota this 5th day of May 2026./s/ Elizabeth WalkupTransmission Account Rep and Technical SpecialistXcel Energy Services Inc.414 Nicollet Mall, 414-6Minneapolis, MN 55401 Phone: (612) 216-8223Email: elizabeth.a.walkup@xcelenergy.comA

value.Title: DEPARTMENT OF ENERGY AWARDS $36M TO EERC FOR ENHANCED OIL RECOVERY PROGRAM
value.Overview: US Fed News, 404words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJG-9K93-RV4W-M28R-00000-00DEPARTMENT OF ENERGY AWARDS $36M TO EERC FOR ENHANCED OIL RECOVERY PROGRAM2026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:08:30ZLexisNexisDEPARTMENT OF ENERGY AWARDS $36M TO EERC FOR ENHANCED OIL RECOVERY PROGRAM

WASHINGTON, May 8 -- Sen. Kevin Cramer issued the following press release:

Abundant oil and gas reserves, paired with first industry-leading fracking technology and affordable, reliable electricity, has put North Dakota at the forefront of U.S. energy dominance. The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) is capitalizing on this advantage by investing $36 million to support the University of North Dakota's Energy & Environmental Research Center's (EERC) work improving enhanced oil recovery technologies in the Bakken formation.

"North Dakota is a model for the rest of the country and demonstrates energy production and energy innovation go hand in hand," said U.S. Senator Kevin Cramer (R-ND). "This investment recognizes years of work by the EERC, industry partners, and state leaders to unlock more of the Bakken's enormous potential while utilizing captured carbon dioxide productively. We know the Bakken still holds billions of barrels of oil just out of reach of our current technology, but through the combined efforts of the Department of Energy and industry partners, those barrels of oil will enhance our national security and increase our economic growth. While there is still more work to be done, this announcement represents a commitment to the future of North Dakota's oil industry-and a promise to America we will not step away from the resources God gave us."

Initial Bakken development only recovers approximately 10-15% of the oil in place. While partnerships between North Dakota and industry have made progress toward recovering additional oil, Cramer led efforts (https://www.cramer.senate.gov/news/press-releases/north-dakota-delegation-energy-dept-submits-report-sandias-crude-oil-characterization-research) to correct leftist narratives regarding Bakken crude and leverage DOE dollars toward recovering the residual oil.

This federal investment includes $36 million from DOE's Hydrocarbons and Geothermal Energy Office, along with $9 million in cost-share funding from the EERC and project partners for one of the six pilot projects.

value.Title: North Dakota oil technology could aid geothermal energy development
value.Overview: Elko Daily Free Press (Nevada), ELKODAILY.COM, 608words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHM-S4M3-RW69-V1TJ-00000-00North Dakota oil technology could aid geothermal energy development2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:59:04ZLexisNexisELKODAILY.COMNorth Dakota oil technology could aid geothermal energy developmentJACOB ORLEDGE North Dakota Monitor

North Dakota researchers believe fracking technology could be the key to introducing geothermal energy to the state's electrical grid, but cautioned there are still many unknowns about its feasibility.

One of those unknowns is whether new geothermal technologies under development will prove to be cost-effective enough in North Dakota's underground rock formations, according to a report presented last week by the Energy and Environmental Research Center.

"There's a lot more work to be done on feasibility for power generation potential," said Tyler Hamman, assistant vice president for strategic partnerships with the EERC, during a presentation to the North Dakota Industrial Commission.

Geothermal energy is produced by drilling underground to rock formations with natural heat, the North Dakota Monitor reported. That heat can be extracted using various technologies, typically by extracting water from adjacent reservoirs or fluids that have been injected, and used to produce energy on the surface.

Hamman said geothermal energy is already being produced in North Dakota by heat pumps for residences. Around 90% of the commercial geothermal energy produced in the U.S. is in California and Nevada, he said. Some compressor stations stations along the Northern Border natural gas pipeline are using heat transfer techniques to utilize waste heat from the stations and that technology can also be used in geothermal energy applications, Hamman said."It's actually surprising how much of this there is," said Attorney General Drew Wrigley, one of three members of the Industrial Commission.

There are new technologies under development that could make commercial geothermal energy for widespread power generation in North Dakota a reality. Some of these are being tested in western states like Utah.

While the underground temperatures in western North Dakota may be hot enough for these new geothermal technologies, Hamman said the tightness of the rock formations could pose a challenge if water can't efficiently pass through to absorb the heat.

The solution may lie with the technologies, workforce and expertise used in North Dakota by the oil and gas industry, Hamman said. The rock formations can be fractured, or fracked, to create enough permeability for water to pass through and absorb the heat before being brought to the surface.

"It's something that was previously cost-prohibitive, though with advancements in oil and gas technology, and obviously fracking, it's brought that cost curve down to the point where it's being looked at as a way to expand our geothermal resource," Hamman said. "Where we're at in terms of enhanced geothermal is really where we were at the start of the shale revolution, in terms of trying to prove out the technology."

He suggested several policy recommendations to encourage further development of geothermal energy in North Dakota. These include refining the regulatory framework governing the energy source, studying rock samples in the state's core library for thermal conductivity and other characteristics, as well as drilling exploratory wells to obtain samples of rock formations that are even deeper underground.

Ultimately a pilot project using realistic operating conditions would help to gather data on what the costs and efficiency of geothermal for power generation might be, Hamman said.

Another finding of the study has been the reuse of unproductive oil wells that have already been drilled does not offer a significant financial benefit.

"By the time you reenter that well, drill it deeper into the interior geothermal heat source, your cost savings are pretty minimal, " Hamman said.

urn:contentItem:6JHT-Y9P3-S8SM-536V-00000-00Xcel Energy to spend $12 Million to occupy 37,400 square feet of space in Amarillo Texas.2026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:08:30ZLexisNexisXcel Energy to spend $12 Million to occupy 37,400 square feet of space in Amarillo Texas.Craig Etkin

Amarillo, Texas - According to state and local development sources, Xcel Energy plans to invest $12 Million to build out 37,400 square feet of new space in Amarillo. The company plans to occupy the new space at 4701 Parkside Drive in Amarillo, on or about July 1, 2027. According to the company website, As a leading electric and natural gas energy company, we offer a comprehensive portfolio of energy-related products and services to 3.4 million electricity customers and 1.9 million natural gas customers across our eight states: Colorado, Michigan, Minnesota, New Mexico, North Dakota, South Dakota, Texas and Wisconsin. Our workforce of more than 12,000 is rising to the challenge of a dynamic, constantly-changing utility industry. One that requires us to be even more customer focused, forward thinking and productive while remaining committed to meeting our customers fundamental need for safe, reliable, affordable energy. Do you have a passion for renewable energy, like wind or solar? Or care about giving back to the community? Youll be in a position to directly impact our energy future. At Xcel Energy, youll be challenged, respected and rewarded. Youll find an ethical team committed to excellence, safety and environmental stewardship. A dynamic company where youll have meaningful work that makes a difference.

To learn more about Xcel Energy, visit http://www.xcelenergy.com/

Company Contact:
Xcel Energy
401 Nicollet Mall
Minneapolis, MN55401
Phone: 612-330-5500
LinkedIn Link: https://www.linkedin.com/company/xcel-energy/

value.Title: Trump administration eases curbs on industries, seeks to boost drilling, mining on taxpayer-owned land
value.Overview: Basic Materials & Resources Monitor Worldwide, 396words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJP-44C3-S4RP-X2D6-00000-00Trump administration eases curbs on industries, seeks to boost drilling, mining on taxpayer-owned land2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisTrump administration eases curbs on industries, seeks to boost drilling, mining on taxpayer-owned land

The Interior Department on Tuesday said it is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land. The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10 per cent of land in the US. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.

Rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres of land

But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land - preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.

Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the "multiple use" mandate for Interior Department lands, by catapulting the "non-use" of federal lands - meaning restoration leases - to a position of prominence.

Trump has pursued flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production

The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.

The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months cancelled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

value.Title: Trump administration eases curbs on industries

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK4-7463-RSB6-C2WT-00000-00Trump administration eases curbs on industries2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisTrump administration eases curbs on industriesFnF Correspondent

The Interior Department on Tuesday said it is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land. The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10 per cent of land in the US. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.

Rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres of land

But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land - preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.

Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the "multiple use" mandate for Interior Department lands, by catapulting the "non-use" of federal lands - meaning restoration leases - to a position of prominence.

Trump has pursued flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production

The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.

The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months cancelled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

urn:contentItem:6JHV-RSJ3-S0JT-N153-00000-00Researchers from University of Oklahoma Describe Findings in Renewable Energy (The Role of Environmental NGOs in the Renewable Energy-Environmental Interface)2026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:08:30ZLexisNexisENERGY - RENEWABLE ENERGYResearchers from University of Oklahoma Describe Findings in Renewable Energy (The Role of Environmental NGOs in the Renewable Energy-Environmental Interface)


2026 MAY 08 (NewsRx) -- By a News Reporter-Staff News Editor at Energy Daily News -- Researchers detail new data in renewable energy. According to news reporting from Norman, Oklahoma, by NewsRx journalists, research stated, "Nongovernmental organizations (NGOs) play an important role in the interface between business, government and society, including serving as a link between diverse stakeholders, amplifying public visibility, and serving as a watch dog."


Our news editors obtained a quote from the research from University of Oklahoma: "This research seeks to understand the involvement and experience of environmental NGO (ENGO) staff members in the environmental planning of utility-scale wind and solar projects. We conducted 19 one-hour interviews with individuals representing 13 ENGOs which were located in or had projects within North Dakota, South Dakota, Nebraska, Kansas, Iowa, Oklahoma, and Texas. We found that, overall, engagement with the renewable energy industry was mixed, with some organizations being very involved and others having limited to no engagement. Participants also shared positive as well as more challenging engagement experiences they have had."
According to the news editors, the research concluded: "Overall, ENGOs see a number of potential opportunities to engage more in renewable energy planning, particularly in collaboration with renewable energy developers, to move renewable energy deployment forward while balancing land use and environmental concerns."
For more information on this research see: The Role of Environmental NGOs in the Renewable Energy-Environmental Interface. Land, 2026,15(4):684. (Land - http://www.mdpi.com/journal/land). The publisher for Land is MDPI AG.
A free version of this journal article is available at https://doi.org/10.3390/land15040684.
Our news journalists report that additional information may be obtained by contacting Claire Burch, Department of Geography and Environmental Sustainability, University of Oklahoma, Norman, OK 73019-1007, United States. Additional authors for this research include Rebecca Loraamm.
Keywords for this news article include: University of Oklahoma, Norman, Oklahoma, United States, North and Central America, Energy, Oil & Gas, Renewable Energy.
Our reports deliver fact-based news of research and discoveries from around the world. Copyright 2026, NewsRx LLC

urn:contentItem:6JJV-PFH3-RX76-S1V5-00000-00Trump ends conservation use rule for public lands2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisTrump ends conservation use rule for public landsIndia

India, May 13 -- The Interior Department is cancelling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.

The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10 per cent of land in the US. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling. But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land - preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands. Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the "multiple use" mandate for Interior Department lands, by catapulting the "non-use" of federal lands - meaning restoration leases - to a position of prominence.

The federal Government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states, including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidised under Biden.

The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published on Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months cancelled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves - such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy - across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometres). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and, for more than a century, has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

urn:contentItem:6JJH-6C93-RXCN-Y4NG-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisBUSINESS NEWSDOMESTIC NEWSPOLITICAL NEWSSTATE AND REGIONALTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public landsMATTHEW BROWNAssociated PressBILLINGS, Mont.

BILLINGS, Mont. (AP) — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.

The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.

But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.

Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands, by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence.

The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.

The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

urn:contentItem:6JJH-6BH3-RXCN-Y4GB-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisBUSINESS NEWSDOMESTIC NEWSPOLITICAL NEWSSTATE AND REGIONALTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public landsMATTHEW BROWNAssociated PressBILLINGS, Mont.

BILLINGS, Mont. (AP) — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.

The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.

But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.

Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands, by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence.

The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.

The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

urn:contentItem:6JJN-9KW3-RT1Y-C255-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisBUSINESS,INTERNATIONALTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public landsMatthew BrownThe Associated Press

BILLINGS, Mont. (AP) — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.

The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.

But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.

Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Bobby McEnaney with the Natural Resources Defense Council said repealing the rule ”means less protection for the clean drinking water, less protection for endangered wildlife that depend on healthy habitat, and less accountability when corporations leave these landscapes damaged and degraded."

In documents released Monday, administration officials said it exceeded the land bureau’s authority for outside parties to be allowed to obtain conservation leases.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence.

“This action provides greater clarity and predictability for independent oil and natural gas producers—many of whom rely on consistent permitting and leasing processes to operate efficiently and invest in domestic energy supply,” Dan Naatz with the Independent Petroleum Association of America said in a statement.

The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.

The repeal is effective 30 days after it is published in the Federal Register, which was scheduled for Tuesday.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

urn:contentItem:6JJH-67W3-RXCN-Y3PX-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisBUSINESS NEWSDOMESTIC NEWSPOLITICAL NEWSSTATE AND REGIONALTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public landsMATTHEW BROWNAssociated PressBILLINGS, Mont.

BILLINGS, Mont. (AP) — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.

The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.

But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.

Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands, by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence.

The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.

The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

urn:contentItem:6JJM-5YX3-RT70-T28K-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisIRAN NEWSTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public landsMatthew Brown

The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.

The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.

But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land - preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.

Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the "multiple use" mandate for Interior Department lands, by catapulting the "non-use" of federal lands - meaning restoration leases - to a position of prominence.

The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.

The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves - such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy - across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

urn:contentItem:6JJK-CTM3-SCTW-63FD-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisBUSINESS NEWSDOMESTIC NEWSPOLITICAL NEWSSTATE AND REGIONALTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public landsMATTHEW BROWNAssociated PressBILLINGS, Mont.

BILLINGS, Mont. — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.

The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.

But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.

Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Bobby McEnaney with the Natural Resources Defense Council said repealing the rule ”means less protection for the clean drinking water, less protection for endangered wildlife that depend on healthy habitat, and less accountability when corporations leave these landscapes damaged and degraded."

In documents released Monday, administration officials said it exceeded the land bureau’s authority for outside parties to be allowed to obtain conservation leases.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence.

“This action provides greater clarity and predictability for independent oil and natural gas producers—many of whom rely on consistent permitting and leasing processes to operate efficiently and invest in domestic energy supply,” Dan Naatz with the Independent Petroleum Association of America said in a statement.

The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.

The repeal is effective 30 days after it is published in the Federal Register, which was scheduled for Tuesday.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

urn:contentItem:6JJK-CSY3-SCTW-62K4-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisBUSINESS NEWSDOMESTIC NEWSPOLITICAL NEWSSTATE AND REGIONALTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public landsMATTHEW BROWNAssociated PressBILLINGS, Mont.

BILLINGS, Mont. (AP) — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.

The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.

But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.

Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Bobby McEnaney with the Natural Resources Defense Council said repealing the rule ”means less protection for the clean drinking water, less protection for endangered wildlife that depend on healthy habitat, and less accountability when corporations leave these landscapes damaged and degraded."

In documents released Monday, administration officials said it exceeded the land bureau’s authority for outside parties to be allowed to obtain conservation leases.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence.

“This action provides greater clarity and predictability for independent oil and natural gas producers—many of whom rely on consistent permitting and leasing processes to operate efficiently and invest in domestic energy supply,” Dan Naatz with the Independent Petroleum Association of America said in a statement.

The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.

The repeal is effective 30 days after it is published in the Federal Register, which was scheduled for Tuesday.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

value.Title: Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands

urn:contentItem:6JJK-CTR3-SCTW-63G9-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisBUSINESS NEWSDOMESTIC NEWSPOLITICAL NEWSSTATE AND REGIONALTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public landsMATTHEW BROWNAssociated PressBILLINGS, Mont.

BILLINGS, Mont. (AP) — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.

The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.

But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.

Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Bobby McEnaney with the Natural Resources Defense Council said repealing the rule ”means less protection for the clean drinking water, less protection for endangered wildlife that depend on healthy habitat, and less accountability when corporations leave these landscapes damaged and degraded."

In documents released Monday, administration officials said it exceeded the land bureau’s authority for outside parties to be allowed to obtain conservation leases.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence.

“This action provides greater clarity and predictability for independent oil and natural gas producers—many of whom rely on consistent permitting and leasing processes to operate efficiently and invest in domestic energy supply,” Dan Naatz with the Independent Petroleum Association of America said in a statement.

The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.

The repeal is effective 30 days after it is published in the Federal Register, which was scheduled for Tuesday.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration. Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands, by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands meaning restoration leases to a position of prominence. The federal government’s vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden. The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance. It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden’s administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota. In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

value.Title: Manitoba Hydro won't identify companies vying to build wind farms - even as some identify themselves
value.Overview: CBC (News), RENEWABLE ENERGY NEWS & UTILITIES NEWS, 512words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJN-6BF3-S7R6-314M-00000-00Manitoba Hydro won't identify companies vying to build wind farms - even as some identify themselves2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:08:30ZLexisNexisRENEWABLE ENERGY NEWS & UTILITIES NEWSManitoba Hydro won't identify companies vying to build wind farms - even as some identify themselves

Manitoba Hydro won't reveal the identities of the companies under consideration to build a series of proposed Indigenous-led wind farms - even as some of those companies have identified themselves.

The provincial Crown corporation won't even reveal how many companies it deemed eligible to submit proposals to build these wind farms, which Hydro is relying upon to generate 600 megawatts of electricity.

Manitoba Hydro issued a formal search in 2025 for companies qualified to build wind farms. The projects must be at least 51 per cent Indigenous-owned under a program announced by the province's NDP government in 2024.

Although Hydro's search for qualified companies has closed, the Crown corporation said it cannot disclose how many passed this bar and are now eligible to submit proposals to actually build the wind farms. The deadline to submit is July.

"I can't provide the number of proponents yet," Hydro spokesperson Peter Chura said Monday in a statement.

"We will identify all the proponents after they all have signed on to the framework agreement, which we expect will be complete in June."

Hydro's statement comes after some of the prospective proponents have gone public with their wind-farm plans on their own.

The Manitoba Métis Federation, for example, is planning to build 30 to 35 windmills southeast of Riding Mountain National Park through a partnership between the MMF's Red River Métis Power Corporation and the U.K.-based Renewable Energy Systems, one of the world's largest green-energy companies.

Renewable Energy Systems has been securing land in the rural municipalities of Minto-Odanah and Rosedale for two years, the MMF's Jack Park told CBC News.

The company held an open house about the project in Polonia, northwest of Neepawa, in April.

While the MMF-Renewable Energy Systems partnership has gone public, Progressive Conservative Hydro critic Lauren Stone urged Manitoba Hydro to be more transparent with Manitobans about other wind-farm partnerships.

"These kinds of long-term deals are being negotiated behind closed doors, and there's little - if any - room to examine community concerns, environmental impact and possible economic benefits to communities," Stone said Monday in a statement.

Adrien Sala, the NDP minister responsible for Manitoba Hydro, said that will happen eventually.

"By design, the government and Manitoba Hydro cannot comment on procurement efforts that are still ongoing in order to ensure the integrity of process," Sala said in a statement. "However, Hydro expects that proponents will be made public prior to the RFPs [requests for proposals] being awarded."

Sala said he expects the first shovels in the ground on the new wind farms during the summer of 2027. Hydro, however, does not expect the wind farms to be complete until 2035.

Manitoba's Climate Action Team, a non-profit environmental organization, said this pace is insufficient for this province to meet the demand for more electricity.

"It is concerning the projects are moving so slowly," said Laura Cameron, a Climate Action Team director, said in a statement.
